steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank sits inside the sentence "improvements in efficiency often correlate negatively with resource ______ efficiency gains, lowering the cost of use, may increase demand...". What comes before the blank is an independent clause; what comes after is also an independent clause. The blank must supply punctuation that (1) closes the first clause and (2) separates it from the second. The word "though" is a conjunctive adverb that must attach to one side.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'conservation; though': Puts the semicolon before "though", leaving "though efficiency gains..." to start the second clause. "Though" is a conjunctive adverb here, not a subordinating conjunction, so this creates a grammatical oddity and is not the SAT's preferred placement. ✗
  • Choice B — 'conservation, though,': Uses only commas on both sides of "though". This leaves two independent clauses joined by nothing stronger than a comma, producing a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'conservation, though': A single comma before "though" without any stronger stop after it. Again, only a comma separates the two independent clauses — comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — 'conservation, though;': The comma sets off "though" as a trailing parenthetical at the end of the first clause, and the semicolon then properly joins the two independent clauses. Both sides of the semicolon are complete sentences. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
